name: Supay
description: Supay is the Incan god of the underworld, death, and demons. He is often associated with the devil in syncretic religions formed after the Spanish conquest. He rules over Ukhu Pacha, the Incan underworld, a dark and cold place where the spirits of the dead reside. Supay is not inherently evil, but rather a powerful force who maintains the balance between life and death. He is often depicted as a trickster figure, capable of both benevolence and malevolence. Miners and others who work in dangerous environments would often make offerings to Supay to appease him and ensure their safety, particularly during festivals like Dia de los Muertos where syncretic beliefs are prominent. He is also associated with fertility, but a more primal and untamed form than that of other Incan deities. While feared, Supay was also respected for his power and influence over the natural order, especially the cycles of life and death. His image and role have evolved over time, influenced by both indigenous beliefs and Christian doctrines, leading to a complex and multifaceted understanding of this powerful deity. He is a significant figure in understanding the Incan worldview and their relationship with the afterlife.
subtitle: Ruler of the Incan Underworld

keyMyths.title: The Pact with Supay
keyMyths.description: Miners often make pacts with Supay, offering sacrifices in exchange for safety and wealth within the mines. Breaking these pacts is said to bring misfortune and even death to the miner and their families.

keyMyths.title: Supay's Dance
keyMyths.description: During Dia de los Muertos, dancers dressed as Supay perform in the streets, representing the deity's presence and power over the dead. These dances are a vibrant expression of syncretic religious beliefs.

keyMyths.title: The Creation of Ukhu Pacha
keyMyths.description: Some myths describe Supay as being responsible for creating Ukhu Pacha, the underworld, as a place for the spirits of the dead to reside and where the seeds of new life are nurtured.

sources.author: Gary Urton
sources.work: At the Crossroads of the Earth and the Sky: An Andean Cosmology
sources.description: Discusses Incan cosmology and the role of the underworld.
sources.text: Discusses Incan cosmology and the role of the underworld.

sources.author: Sabine MacCormack
sources.work: Religion in the Andes: Vision and Imagination in Early Colonial Peru
sources.description: Explores the transformation of indigenous beliefs under Spanish rule.
sources.text: Explores the transformation of indigenous beliefs under Spanish rule.

sources.author: Various
sources.description: Collection of stories and myths passed down through generations.
sources.text: Collection of stories and myths passed down through generations.

cross_cultural_parallels.name: Lord of the Afterlife | Egyptian Mythology

worshipPractices.name: Offerings
worshipPractices.description: Offerings, including coca leaves, alcohol (chicha), and sometimes animal sacrifices (llamas or guinea pigs), were made to appease Supay and seek his favor or prevent misfortune. These offerings were often conducted in caves or other liminal spaces considered entrances to the underworld.

worshipPractices.name: Ritual Celebrations
worshipPractices.description: Rituals involving music, dance, and storytelling were performed to honor Supay and acknowledge his power. These rituals often took place during periods of transition, such as harvests or solstices, when the boundary between the living world and the underworld was believed to be thinner.

festivals.name: Anchanchu
festivals.description: Though primarily associated with the Aymara people, the festival of Anchanchu, celebrated in February, shares similarities with Supay worship. It involves rituals to appease subterranean spirits and ensure good fortune for the coming year.

festivals.name: Dia de los Muertos (Syncretic)
festivals.description: In syncretic Andean Catholicism, Supay is sometimes associated with Dia de los Muertos (Day of the Dead). While not directly worshipped, the festival recognizes the spirits of the deceased and reflects Andean beliefs about the afterlife and the importance of honoring ancestors.

cultural.modernLegacy: Supay's image persists in Andean folklore and popular culture, often depicted in theatrical performances and dances during festivals. He is sometimes syncretized with the Christian devil, particularly in mining communities where he is seen as the owner of the mines and must be appeased for safe and successful extraction. The concept of Supay continues to influence Andean cosmology and beliefs about the afterlife.

extendedContent.title: Origins & Birth
extendedContent.content: The exact origins of Supay are somewhat obscured by the passage of time and the suppression of indigenous beliefs following the Spanish conquest. He is deeply rooted in pre-Columbian Andean cosmology, predating the Incan empire, with similar deities appearing in various regional cultures. Supay is not typically associated with a specific birth or creation myth in the same way as other Incan gods like Viracocha (the creator god) or Inti (the sun god). Rather, he is understood as an eternal and inherent force of the cosmos, existing as long as there has been death and an underworld. His domain, Ukhu Pacha, is an essential part of the Incan worldview, representing the cyclical nature of life, death, and rebirth. Supay's power stems from this primal connection to the earth and the spirits of the ancestors who reside within it. He is a constant reminder of mortality and the inevitable journey to the underworld.

extendedContent.title: Major Myths & Stories
extendedContent.content: Unlike some deities with extensive myth cycles, Supay's stories are often fragmented and interwoven with local traditions. He doesn't have a central, overarching narrative. Instead, his role is typically manifested in tales of human interaction with the underworld and its spirits. Miners often tell stories of encountering Supay within the mines, where he demands offerings and respect in exchange for safe passage and rich veins of ore. Failure to appease him can result in accidents, illness, or even death. Other stories depict Supay as a tempter, luring individuals into the underworld with promises of wealth or power, only to trap them in his domain. He is also sometimes portrayed as a judge of souls, determining their fate in the afterlife based on their actions in life. These narratives reinforce Supay's role as a powerful and unpredictable force, deserving of both respect and caution.

extendedContent.title: Worship & Cult
extendedContent.content: Formal worship of Supay was suppressed after the Spanish conquest, but elements of his veneration persisted in syncretic forms. Traditional practices involved offerings of coca leaves, chicha (a fermented corn beverage), and animal sacrifices to appease him and ensure safety from misfortune, especially in dangerous occupations like mining. Rituals were often performed in caves, considered entrances to the underworld. Shamans or priests acted as intermediaries between the living and Supay, interpreting his will and conducting ceremonies. While direct temples dedicated solely to Supay were uncommon, sacred sites associated with the underworld, such as caves and burial grounds, served as focal points for his worship. Modern-day manifestations of Supay worship are often intertwined with Catholic traditions, particularly during festivals like Dia de los Muertos, where offerings are made to deceased ancestors, reflecting a continued belief in the power of the underworld and its ruler.

extendedContent.title: Legacy & Influence
extendedContent.content: Supay's legacy extends beyond the purely religious sphere, influencing Andean folklore, art, and social customs. He remains a potent symbol of the underworld and the mysteries of death. In many Andean communities, particularly those involved in mining, Supay is still regarded as the owner of the mines and must be appeased to ensure the safety and prosperity of the workers. His image often appears in theatrical performances and dances during festivals, serving as a reminder of the power of the underworld. The syncretic association of Supay with the Christian devil has also shaped the perception of evil and temptation in Andean cultures. Despite the efforts of colonial authorities to eradicate indigenous beliefs, Supay's enduring presence demonstrates the resilience of Andean cosmology and the continued importance of the underworld in shaping cultural identity and worldview.

shortDescription: Supay is the Incan god of the underworld, death, and demons. He rules Ukhu Pacha, the Incan underworld, and is often syncretized with the Christian devil.
